hint: mean = Σx/n; median = middle value; s² = Σ(x−x̄)²/(n−1); mean > median ⇒ right skew.
front:
A sample of 7 claim amounts (£000s) is: 2, 4, 4, 5, 7, 9, 11.

(i) Calculate the sample mean and median. **[2]**
(ii) Calculate the sample variance (n−1 divisor) and standard deviation. **[3]**
(iii) State whether the data appear skewed, with a reason. **[1]**
model_answer:
**Approach:** mean = Σx/n; median = middle ordered value; s² = Σ(x−x̄)²/(n−1); compare mean and median for skew.

**Solution:**
(i) Mean = 42/7 = 6. Median = 5 (the 4th of 7 ordered values).
(ii) Deviations from 6: −4, −2, −2, −1, 1, 3, 5; their squares 16, 4, 4, 1, 1, 9, 25 sum to 60. s² = 60/(7−1) = 10, so s = √10 = 3.16.
(iii) Mean (6) > median (5), so the data are slightly positively (right) skewed.

hint: quantile / IQR for spread; boxplot for shape & outliers; logical-sum to count outliers.
front:
(CS1B / R) Claim amounts are in a numeric vector `x`.

(i) Write R to obtain the quartiles and the interquartile range. **[2]**
(ii) Write R to draw a boxplot and say what it reveals. **[2]**
(iii) Write R to count values beyond 1.5×IQR above Q3. **[1]**
model_answer:
**Approach:** `quantile`/`IQR` for spread; `boxplot` for shape and outliers; a logical sum counts outliers.

**Solution:**
(i)
```r
quantile(x)            # min, Q1, median, Q3, max
IQR(x)                 # Q3 - Q1
```
(ii)
```r
boxplot(x)
```
The box shows the median and IQR; the whiskers and any plotted points reveal the spread, skew and outliers.
(iii)
```r
sum(x > quantile(x, 0.75) + 1.5 * IQR(x))
```

hint: sd is in the data's units; +c shifts mean only; ×a scales mean by a and sd by |a|.
front:
(i) Define the sample standard deviation and explain why it is often preferred to the variance for reporting. **[2]**
(ii) Explain the effect on the mean and standard deviation of (a) adding a constant c to every value, and (b) multiplying every value by a. **[3]**
model_answer:
**Approach:** Recall the units argument and the location/scale transformation rules.

**Solution:**
(i) The standard deviation is the square root of the variance, s = √s². It is preferred for reporting because it is in the **same units** as the data, whereas the variance is in squared units.
(ii) (a) Adding c: the mean increases by c; the standard deviation is **unchanged** (a shift does not affect spread). (b) Multiplying by a: the mean is multiplied by a; the standard deviation is multiplied by |a| (and the variance by a²).

mark_scheme.point: Preferred: same units as the data

front:
(i) Define the Pearson correlation coefficient and state its range. **[2]**
(ii) State when Spearman's rank correlation is preferred to Pearson's. **[2]**
(iii) State one limitation common to both. **[1]**
model_answer:
**Approach:** Recall the definitions, assumptions and shared limitation of the two correlation measures.

**Solution:**
(i) Pearson's r = Cov(X,Y)/(σ_X σ_Y) (sample form S_xy/√(S_xx S_yy)); it measures **linear** association and lies in [−1, 1].
(ii) Spearman's (rank) correlation is preferred when the relationship is monotonic but non-linear, when the data are ordinal, or when there are outliers / non-Normality — working on ranks makes it more robust.
(iii) Neither implies causation, and both can miss a strong non-monotonic relationship (e.g. a U-shape can give r ≈ 0).

mark_scheme.point: Pearson r = Cov/(σ_X σ_Y), measures linear association

hint: S_xx = Σx² − (Σx)²/n etc.; r = S_xy/√(S_xx S_yy).
front:
For paired data: Σx = 30, Σy = 40, Σx² = 220, Σy² = 360, Σxy = 270, n = 5.

(i) Calculate S_xx, S_yy and S_xy. **[3]**
(ii) Calculate the Pearson correlation coefficient. **[2]**
(iii) Interpret it. **[1]**
model_answer:
**Approach:** S_xx = Σx² − (Σx)²/n (and similarly S_yy, S_xy); r = S_xy/√(S_xx S_yy).

**Solution:**
(i) S_xx = 220 − 30²/5 = 220 − 180 = 40. S_yy = 360 − 40²/5 = 360 − 320 = 40. S_xy = 270 − (30·40)/5 = 270 − 240 = 30.
(ii) r = S_xy/√(S_xx S_yy) = 30/√(40·40) = 30/40 = 0.75.
(iii) A fairly strong positive linear association between x and y.

hint: Grouped mean uses class midpoints; modal class = highest frequency; median class holds the middle value.
front:
Claims are grouped: [0, 10): 8 claims; [10, 20): 12; [20, 30): 5.

(i) Estimate the mean claim using midpoints. **[2]**
(ii) State one reason the grouped-data mean is only an approximation. **[1]**
(iii) Identify the modal class and the class containing the median. **[2]**
model_answer:
**Approach:** Use class midpoints for the mean; modal class = highest frequency; the median class contains the middle (here 13th of 25) value.

**Solution:**
(i) Midpoints 5, 15, 25; mean ≈ (8·5 + 12·15 + 5·25)/25 = 345/25 = 13.8.
(ii) It assumes every observation lies at its class midpoint, which is only approximate.
(iii) Modal class = [10, 20) (highest frequency, 12). With n = 25 the median is the 13th value; cumulative frequencies are 8 then 20, so the median lies in [10, 20).

hint: Cov = E[XY] − E[X]E[Y]; corr = Cov/√(VarX·VarY); Var(X+Y) = VarX + VarY + 2Cov.
front:
Random variables X and Y have E[X] = 1, E[Y] = 2, E[XY] = 3, Var(X) = 2, Var(Y) = 5.

(i) Calculate Cov(X, Y). **[2]**
(ii) Calculate the correlation coefficient. **[2]**
(iii) Calculate Var(X + Y). **[2]**
model_answer:
**Approach:** Cov(X,Y) = E[XY] − E[X]E[Y]; corr = Cov/√(VarX·VarY); Var(X+Y) = VarX + VarY + 2Cov(X,Y).

**Solution:**
(i) Cov(X,Y) = E[XY] − E[X]E[Y] = 3 − 1·2 = 1.
(ii) corr = 1/√(2·5) = 1/√10 = 0.316.
(iii) Var(X+Y) = 2 + 5 + 2·1 = 9.

hint: ANOVA tests equality of ≥3 means; assumptions independence/Normality/equal variance; equivalent to regression on a factor.
front:
(i) State the purpose of one-way ANOVA and its null hypothesis. **[2]**
(ii) State the assumptions of one-way ANOVA. **[2]**
(iii) Explain how ANOVA relates to a regression with a categorical predictor. **[1]**
model_answer:
**Approach:** Recall ANOVA's purpose, assumptions, and its equivalence to regression on dummy variables.

**Solution:**
(i) One-way ANOVA tests whether the means of three or more groups are equal; H₀: μ₁ = μ₂ = … = μ_k.
(ii) Observations are independent; Normally distributed within each group; with equal variance across groups (homoscedasticity).
(iii) One-way ANOVA is equivalent to a linear regression using the group as a categorical predictor (dummy variables); the regression F-test for the factor is the ANOVA F-test.

mark_scheme.point: Equivalent to regression on a categorical predictor
